content: The human language-learning concept of reflexion (as a taught vocabulary and metacognitive skill) can be framed as a blueprint for grounded LLM self-reflection mechanisms that are tied to embodied usage contexts rather than abstract logical inconsistency. Teaching LLMs to explicitly 'practice reflexion' the way high school students practice the vocabulary and metacognitive habit of reflection can create more robust, generalizable self-reflection capabilities that adapt to domain-specific tasks.
